name: Barcino
classification_notes: Barcino is described as a sans-serif inspired by geometric forms, placing it squarely in the geometric sans-serif subcategory with clean, rational proportions.
summary: An elegant geometric sans-serif from Typerepublic, named after the Roman colony that became Barcelona, with five weights and an extensive set of OpenType features.
description: Barcino draws its name and spirit from the Roman origins of Barcelona, channelling geometric forms into a clean, contemporary sans-serif. Five weights run from Thin to Bold, supported by an unusually thorough OpenType feature set including sixteen stylistic sets, oldstyle figures, discretionary ligatures and a slashed zero. The result is a versatile workhorse that retains enough character for editorial and branding work across the weight range.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Barcino as an elegant sans-serif typeface inspired by geometric forms and proportion, named for the ancient Roman colony at the heart of modern Barcelona.

name: Aloma
classification_notes: Aloma is described as Neoclassical in style with classical Roman proportions and high stroke contrast, drawing on Deco lettering and the Catalan Noucentisme movement, placing it firmly in the neoclassical serif tradition.
summary: A neoclassical serif by Andreu Balius inspired by Mercè Rodoreda's 1938 Catalan novel and the aesthetics of Deco magazine lettering.
description: Designed between 2017 and 2020, the family draws on Art Deco headline lettering and the principles of the Catalan Noucentisme movement, resulting in high-contrast roman proportions with deliberate inconsistencies across certain letterforms. Five weights from Thin to Bold are offered, each with an italic, covering an extensive range of Latin languages. Its sharpness and contrast make it best suited to editorial titling, magazine covers and short display settings rather than extended text.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Aloma as a type family designed for both text and display, inspired by Mercè Rodoreda's novel published in Barcelona in 1938. The design draws on Deco magazine headlines and the Catalan Noucentisme movement, presenting a Neoclassical style with classical Roman proportions and some intentional inconsistencies. It is primarily intended for editorial design such as magazines and catalogues, with particular strength in titling and short introductory texts. The family received a Silver Award at the European Design Awards 2021.

name: Barna
classification_notes: Barna is described as having a simple, rational tone suited to technical and informational content, placing it firmly in the tradition of rational or neo-grotesque sans-serifs designed for extended text use.
summary: A rational sans-serif from Typerepublic, built for long-form and technical reading with condensed widths, stencil display variant and extensive OpenType features.
description: Designed around a simple, rational tone, Barna addresses technical and informational text settings with a full weight range from ExtraLight to ExtraBold, each with matching italics and six condensed weights. The family extends to a stencil display variant that introduces an urban character, while OpenType features include small caps, old-style figures, historical ligatures and two stylistic sets. Its character makes it a capable workhorse for editorial, institutional or publishing contexts where clarity and flexibility are priorities.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Barna as a typeface designed for long-form reading, with a simple and rational tone that suits technical and informational content. The family includes condensed weights and a stencilled display style called Barna Stencil, which adds an urban character. They position it as a true workhorse typeface: flexible and versatile in both style and function, with a comprehensive range of styles and glyphs including small caps and old-style figures.

name: Brossa
classification_notes: Brossa is a sans-serif with a broad width axis spanning Condensed to X-Wide, consistent with the grotesque tradition. The extensive weight and width range, combined with the absence of decorative or humanist features, places it firmly in the grotesque sans category.
summary: A large grotesque sans spanning six widths and nine weights, named after the Catalan poet and visual artist Joan Brossa.
description: Running from Condensed to X-Wide across nine weights from Thin to Black, Brossa offers an unusually broad typographic palette within a single family. Each width comes with a full set of italics, giving designers considerable flexibility across print and screen contexts. OpenType features include case-sensitive forms, ordinals, slashed zero and four stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic named the typeface after Joan Brossa (1919–1998), the Catalan poet, playwright, graphic designer and visual artist who wrote exclusively in Catalan and was a founding figure of the Dau al Set group. Brossa was a pioneer of visual poetry and worked across theatre, cinema, music, magic and the circus, receiving the National Prize for the Visual Arts in 1992 and the National Theatre Prize in 1998.

name: Barna Stencil Rough
classification_notes: A stencil-style display typeface with a deliberately rough, distressed texture intended for urban and protest contexts. The stencil construction and degraded surface place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A rough-textured stencil display family by Andreu Balius, designed as a companion to the Barna family and carrying an unmistakably urban, wall-painted character.
description: Seven styles span regular and condensed widths across three weights, all sharing a degraded, textured surface that references stencilled messages on neighbourhood walls. The family is designed for display use and works alongside the broader Barna family, with extensive Latin language support and a full suite of OpenType features including small caps, oldstyle figures and fractions. The rough treatment gives it a raw, political edge suited to editorial, campaign and street-culture contexts.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Barna Stencil Rough as a display type family created to work in harmony with the Barna family. Its bold stencil style adds an urban character available in both regular and condensed versions, and its rough texture pays tribute to the spirit of neighbourhood walls, where messages shout, rebel and stand out against the norms of conventional life.

name: Barna Stencil
classification_notes: A stencil display typeface with deliberate cut-through forms derived from the Barna family, intended for expressive, high-impact settings rather than continuous text.
summary: A stencil display typeface by Andreu Balius that sits within the broader Barna family, built around cut-through forms that evoke spray-painted urgency.
description: Part of the wider Barna type family, Barna Stencil uses deliberate stencil cuts to push the design into expressive, high-impact territory while retaining visual consistency with its siblings. Six styles span Regular, Bold and ExtraBold across standard and Condensed widths, making it equally suited to large headlines and space-constrained signage. Broad Latin extended language support and OpenType features including small capitals, oldstyle figures and contextual alternates give it practical depth beyond its poster-facing character.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Barna Stencil as the rebel of the Barna family, designed with stencilled cuts to amplify a message and feel like an outburst on the wall. Despite its expressive character it maintains visual consistency with the rest of the Barna family, and includes a condensed version for situations where space is limited but impact is essential.

name: Carmen
classification_notes: High-contrast design with smooth rounded terminals and delicate hairline strokes places it firmly in the neoclassical tradition, akin to Didone but with softer, more organic terminal treatment.
summary: A high-contrast neoclassical serif by Andreu Balius, originally commissioned for a new edition of Prosper Mérimée's novella Carmen, with delicate hairline strokes and gently curved italics.
description: Designed between 2007 and 2008, the typeface takes its character from the romantic myth of its namesake: high stroke contrast, smooth rounded terminals and italics that tilt lightly as though mid-movement. Three weights run from Regular to Black, each with a matching italic, giving the family enough range for display and sustained text setting. It was recognised as Best Typeface of 2008 by Typographica and featured in FontShop's Top Type 2009.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Carmen as a neoclassical, high-contrast design inspired by the myth of the Spanish gypsy woman as popularised by Mérimée. Originally commissioned for a new edition of the romantic tale, it features delicate strokes as fine as golden threads and slightly inclined italics that approach the text like a graceful dance, expressing contrast, passion and refined elegance.

name: Carmen Cyrillic
classification_notes: High-contrast design with smooth rounded terminals and delicate thin strokes, placing it firmly in the neoclassical or Didone tradition. The refined contrast and elegant italic cuts align with late 18th-century rational serif models.
summary: A high-contrast neoclassical serif with Cyrillic support, originally commissioned for a new edition of Mérimée's novel Carmen, recognised as one of Typographica's best typefaces of 2008.
description: Designed by Andreu Balius between 2007 and 2008, with Cyrillic advisory from Ilya Ruderman, the typeface draws on the neoclassical tradition with fine hairline strokes, smooth rounded terminals and a gently inclined italic that the foundry describe as approaching the text like a dance. Cyrillic coverage extends to Russian, Serbian and Ukrainian, alongside a broad Latin character set spanning many European and minority languages. Its literary origins and award recognition from both Typographica and FontShop mark it as a considered text face with a distinct thematic identity.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Carmen Cyrillic as inspired by the myth of the Spanish gypsy woman from Mérimée's novel, originally commissioned for a new edition of that text. They characterise it as a high-contrast neoclassical design with smooth rounded terminals and strokes as fine as golden threads, whose italics feel soft and almost fragile, as if approaching the text in a graceful dance. The typeface supports Russian, Serbian and Ukrainian Cyrillic alongside a comprehensive range of Latin languages.

name: Carmen Display
classification_notes: High-contrast letterforms with soft, rounded terminals and fine hairline strokes place this squarely in the neoclassical tradition, akin to Didone designs but with a softer, more ornamental quality suited to display use.
summary: A high-contrast neoclassical display face by Andreu Balius, originally designed for a new edition of Prosper Mérimée's novel and later adapted as a custom typeface for Victoria's Secret.
description: Drawn with fine hairline strokes, soft rounded terminals and a strong thick-thin contrast, Carmen Display sits within the neoclassical serif tradition while leaning toward the ornamental and sensual. The family comprises just two styles, Regular and Italic, with the italic cut described as gently slanted and almost fragile in character. An extensive Latin language list and OpenType feature set including oldstyle figures, historical ligatures and discretionary ligatures make it more capable than its two-style footprint suggests.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Carmen Display as a neoclassical, high-contrast design with soft rounded terminals and delicate strokes as fine as golden threads, its italics approaching text like a graceful dance. Originally conceived for titling and drop caps in a new edition of Mérimée's Carmen, it was later developed as a custom typeface for Victoria's Secret.

name: Carmen Fiesta
classification_notes: Carmen Fiesta is a flourished, embellished display typeface with decorative letterforms drawn from vintage bullfighting posters and southern Spanish festival typography, placing it firmly in the ornamental display category.
summary: A single-weight ornamental display face by Andreu Balius, drawn from the decorative typography of vintage bullfighting posters and southern Spanish festival announcements.
description: Designed between 2008 and 2009, Carmen Fiesta is the expressive offshoot of Andreu Balius's broader Carmen family, built around the visual language of Andalusian festival graphics. Its embellished stylistic sets amplify the decorative quality, making it suited to poster work, event branding and any context calling for a festive, southern Spanish character. A rich Latin Extended character set covers a wide range of languages, with localised forms for Catalan and Dutch.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Carmen Fiesta, or simply Fiesta for friends, as a flourished style inspired by the ornamental typography of vintage bullfighting posters and southern festival announcements. It features an embellished stylistic set to enhance its decorative impact, and within the Carmen family it plays the role of the expressive, joyful younger cousin — a festive outfit for special occasions.

name: Carmen Fine
classification_notes: High-contrast design with fine hairline strokes, rounded terminals, and a strong axis characteristic of Neoclassical or Didone serifs, positioned as a display cut for headline use.
summary: A high-contrast Neoclassical display serif by Andreu Balius, originally developed as a custom style for Victoria's Secret and released as part of the broader Carmen family.
description: Drawn with hairline strokes, rounded terminals and a strong vertical axis, Carmen Fine sits firmly in the Neoclassical tradition, designed for titles and headings rather than running text. The italic cuts are gently inclined with softened curves, giving the family a delicate, almost fragile quality suited to fashion and luxury contexts. Extensive Latin language support and OpenType features including oldstyle figures, historical forms and two stylistic sets extend its editorial utility.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Carmen Fine as a display style developed as a non-exclusive custom typeface for Victoria's Secret, forming part of the wider Carmen family. The design is characterised as a high-contrast Neoclassical serif with soft rounded terminals and strokes as fine as golden threads, with italics that approach the text like a graceful dance. The typeface was designed by Andreu Balius between 2007 and 2008.

name: Czeska
classification_notes: Czeska is a display typeface directly inspired by woodcut letterforms, with deliberately irregular contours and tactile texture that place it firmly outside conventional text categories. Its handmade, carved quality and expressive rhythm define it as a woodcut display type.
summary: A display typeface drawn from the woodcut letterforms of Czech artist Vojtěch Preissig, with deliberately irregular strokes that turn imperfection into expressive character.
description: Designed by Andreu Balius in 1997 and revised in 2003, Czeska takes the carved, tactile quality of early 20th-century woodcut printing as its direct source. An OpenType glyph alternation feature introduces variation as you type, reinforcing the handmade rhythm without sacrificing legibility. The family covers Regular and Italic styles with an extensive range of characters, ligatures and OpenType features including historical forms and multiple stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Czeska as drawing inspiration from the woodcut prints of Czech artist Vojtěch Preissig (1873–1944). They highlight how each stem and contour carries the tactile richness of carved wood, and that imperfection is a defining strength rather than a flaw. An OpenType feature alternates glyphs automatically as text is set, reinforcing the organic, handmade texture throughout the family.

name: Mecano
classification_notes: Mecano is built on geometric forms with clean, open shapes that evoke mechanical technology and sci-fi aesthetics, placing it firmly in the display category rather than a workhorse geometric sans.
summary: A geometric display face by Andreu Balius that draws on mechanical technology and science fiction aesthetics across five weights.
description: Designed in 2007 by Andreu Balius, Mecano takes its cues from geometric forms associated with mechanical technology, sci-fi films and futurist pop culture. The five weights from UltraLight to Black give it a workable range for titling and editorial use, though no italic or condensed cuts are available. Its clean, spacious letterforms lend themselves to contexts where a retro-futurist or B-movie flavour is appropriate.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Mecano as a typeface inspired by geometric forms whose clean, spacious shapes capture the spirit of mechanical technology, evoking connections to sci-fi films, visions of unpredictable futures, B-movies, the Trekkie universe and contemporary futurist pop culture. They characterise it as offering a playful yet imaginative view of the future, available in five styles from UltraLight to Black.

name: Groundbeat
classification_notes: A modular stencil-inspired display family with square shapes and organic construction, clearly intended for headline use rather than text setting, placing it firmly in the display category with a stencil subcategory.
summary: A stencil-inspired display family by Andreu Balius and Ricard Garcia, evoking the suburban textures and downtempo rhythms of 1990s Trip-Hop.
description: Designed in 2020, Groundbeat blends square modular structure with organic stencil construction to produce a typeface that feels simultaneously solid and fluid. The family spans six styles, each retaining the distinct breaks and gaps of a stencil approach, and is aimed squarely at display use in urban contexts. Its moody, atmospheric character makes it a natural fit for music branding, poster work and editorial headlines where a rhythmic, slightly dark personality is needed.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Groundbeat as a family that captures the spirit of suburban life and reflects the laid-back, breakbeat sounds of the 1990s. They note its downtempo rhythm comes from balancing square shapes with organic stencil-inspired construction, linking it to the dark, rhythmic atmosphere of Trip-Hop. The six-style family is presented as moody and atmospheric, designed for eye-catching display text in urban environments.

name: Groundbeat
classification_notes: Groundbeat is explicitly categorised as a display family with stencil-inspired organic construction and square forms, designed for large-scale urban display use rather than text setting.
summary: A stencil-inspired display family from Andreu Balius and Ricard Garcia, drawing on the dark, rhythmic atmosphere of 1990s trip-hop and breakbeat culture.
description: Six weights from ExtraLight to Black carry a consistent stencil character built on square forms softened by organic, flowing construction. The design takes its cue from Bristol's trip-hop scene, producing a moody, atmospheric feel suited to music, urban culture and event-driven identities. OpenType features include case-sensitive forms, proportional figures, stylistic sets and a slashed zero, extending its utility across display contexts.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Groundbeat as a type family capturing the spirit of suburban life and the laid-back breakbeat sounds of the 1990s. They note that its downtempo rhythm comes from a balance of square structural shapes and an organic stencil-inspired construction, connecting the family to the dark, rhythmic atmosphere of trip-hop from its earliest conception. The family comprises six styles, each preserving the distinct character of the stencil approach, and is positioned as moody and atmospheric for display use in urban environments.

name: Patufet
classification_notes: The specimen and weight names suggest a text-oriented serif family with a range from fine to heavy weights, consistent with a transitional or humanist serif design intended for both text and display use.
summary: A five-weight serif family from Typerepublic with Catalan-inflected naming, spanning from fine text to a heavy display weight, each with matching italics.
description: The family runs across five weights — Finet, Prim, Mig, Gras and Obes — each paired with an italic, giving ten styles in total. The weight names draw on Catalan vocabulary, and the specimen samples include Catalan-language text, suggesting a regional cultural sensibility. OpenType features are extensive, covering oldstyle figures, historical forms, historical ligatures, fractions and multiple stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic present Patufet as a serif family with five weights and matching italics, named using Catalan weight descriptors ranging from Finet (fine) through to Obes (heavy), with a broad OpenType feature set including historical forms, ligatures and figure variants.

name: Patufet Mono
classification_notes: A fixed-pitch monospaced typeface explicitly designed to evoke the feel of a typewriter, with a simple Roman structure and weight variations named in Catalan.
summary: A monospaced typeface with a typewriter character, designed by Andreu Balius and Ricard Garcia and named after a tiny hero from Catalan folk tradition.
description: Five weights from UltraLight (Finet) to an unlabelled heaviest (Obes), each with matching italics, give the family a practical range for editorial and UI work. The design draws on Catalan folktale character Patufet to inform its unpretentious Roman forms, with heavier weights picking up a slightly naïve, rustic quality. OpenType features include oldstyle figures, fractions, slashed zero and case-sensitive forms.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Patufet Mono as the monospaced member of the Patufet family, evoking traditional typewriter aesthetics. It is inspired by Patufet, the tiny protagonist of one of Catalonia's most beloved folktales, rendered as a simple and unassuming Roman design. Weight names follow Catalan descriptors: Finet for UltraLight elegance, Prim for understated lightness, Mig for calm regularity, and Gras for a stronger, slightly naïve boldness.

name: Mercè
classification_notes: A revival of Louis Hoell's 1914 Tages-Antiqua, itself rooted in European Art Nouveau; features wide characters, uneven vertical proportions and increased stroke contrast in a transitional model.
summary: A revival of Louis Hoell's 1914 Tages-Antiqua, reworked by Ricard Garcia with greater elegance and contrast while preserving its Art Nouveau proportions.
description: Originally cut for the Flinsch foundry in Offenbach am Main, Tages-Antiqua circulated in Barcelona as Antigua Mercedes and was rooted in the mainstream Art Nouveau styles of early 20th-century Europe. Garcia's revival retains the wide characters and uneven vertical proportions of Hoell's design while introducing a more contrasted, transitional feel across five weights from Light to Black. The family supports an extensive range of Latin-based languages and includes OpenType features such as historical forms, ordinals and slashed zero.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Mercè as a revival of Tages-Antiqua, designed by Louis Hoell in 1914 for the Flinsch foundry in Offenbach am Main and known in Barcelona as Antigua Mercedes. They note the original's strong Art Nouveau influence, citing its wide characters and uneven vertical proportions, and position Mercè as a reinterpretation that preserves Hoell's spirit while offering a more elegant and contrasted transitional model, created 109 years after the original.

name: Pradell Arabic
classification_notes: The Arabic script component follows the traditional Naskh calligraphic style, a broadly used serif-like script family for literary text; the Latin counterpart is the Pradell serif family, making this a multiscript serif pairing.
summary: A multiscript typeface by Andreu Balius pairing a traditional Naskh-style Arabic with its Latin counterpart, Pradell, for literary text composition.
description: Designed between 2009 and 2013, this typeface draws on the Naskh calligraphic tradition to produce an Arabic script suited to literary typesetting. The family covers extended Arabic including Farsi and Urdu, alongside extended Latin, and is offered in Regular and Bold weights. It won a European Design Award in 2011 and is conceived as a direct companion to the broader Pradell Latin family.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Pradell Arabic as a multiscript typeface that harmoniously combines Latin and Arabic scripts. The Arabic is rooted in the traditional Naskh calligraphic style and designed primarily for literary text composition in Arabic, with support for extended Latin and extended Arabic including Farsi and Urdu. Regular and Bold weights pair directly with the Pradell family as its Latin counterpart.

name: Pradell
classification_notes: Freely inspired by 18th-century Spanish type specimens and the punches of Catalan punchcutter Eudald Pradell, placing it firmly in the transitional serif tradition with historical forms and balanced proportions characteristic of the period.
summary: A transitional serif rooted in research into 18th-century Spanish type specimens, freely inspired by the punches of Catalan punchcutter Eudald Pradell (1721–1788).
description: Designed by Andreu Balius between 1999 and 2003, the family draws on archival study of Spanish type specimens to revive the spirit of Eudald Pradell's punchcutting work. Six styles run from Roman to Bold Italic, with an extensive OpenType feature set covering small capitals, historical forms, oldstyle figures and more, making it well suited to literary and long-form editorial composition. The design was recognised with awards from ATypI, TDC and AEPD, underlining its standing as a serious contribution to book typography.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Pradell as the result of an in-depth research study into 18th-century Spanish type specimens, freely inspired by the punches of the renowned Catalan punchcutter Eudald Pradell (1721–1788). The family is crafted for literary text composition with attention to historical authenticity and contemporary usability, supporting all Latin-based languages and offering high readability at small sizes alongside elegance suited to book and editorial work.

name: Poblet
classification_notes: Poblet is a display type family inspired by the classic Elzevier Condensed, a historical condensed serif form. Its three decorative styles (Solid, Bevel, Diamond) place it firmly in the display category with a strong historical serif lineage.
summary: A three-style condensed display serif drawn from Elzevier Condensed traditions, named after the Cistercian Poblet Abbey in Tarragona, Spain.
description: Designed by Andreu Balius and Ricard Garcia in 2018, the family offers three distinct variants: Solid, Bevel and Diamond, each lending a different decorative register to the same condensed serif skeleton. The forms carry echoes of both Gothic ecclesiastical architecture and Art Deco titling, making the family suited to editorial headlines, packaging and cultural projects. Extensive Latin language support spans hundreds of languages with localised forms for Catalan and Dutch.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Poblet as a display typeface designed for titles and short texts, inspired by the classic Elzevier Condensed. Named after the UNESCO World Heritage Poblet Abbey in Tarragona, Spain, the family aims to reflect the elegance and architectural beauty of the Cistercian monastery's Gothic heritage across three distinct styles, each with its own typographic voice.

name: Salmantina
classification_notes: A narrow, elegant display typeface derived from 16th-century inscriptional lettering on the walls of Salamanca University, rooted in the monumental Roman tradition with a vertical, upright axis and refined modulated strokes.
summary: A display typeface drawn from 16th-century inscriptional lettering on the walls of Salamanca University, first developed in 1997 for the university's signage system and comprehensively revised between 2020 and 2023.
description: Originally created for the signage of Salamanca University campus under the name Universitas Studii Salamantini, the design takes its proportions and weight from narrow monumental Roman lettering that has adorned the university's walls since the 16th century. A major revision completed in 2023 introduced lowercase letters and an extensive set of OpenType alternates, including ten stylistic sets, discretionary ligatures and ordinals. The result is a family of five weights suited to academic, cultural and heritage contexts where historical gravitas is required.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Salmantina as a display typeface inspired by historic lettering found on the walls of Salamanca University since the 16th century. First developed in 1997 for the university's campus signage system, it won the People's Choice Award at the Macromedia Conference in San Francisco that same year. A comprehensive revision carried out between 2020 and 2023 yielded a more refined design with lowercase letters and numerous alternative glyphs, continuing to embody the academic heritage and timeless elegance of its origins.

name: Rioja
classification_notes: Rioja is described by its designer as inspired by geometric and grotesque styles of the early 20th century, combining a clean geometric structure in the uppercase with humanist detailing in the lowercase.
summary: A geometric grotesque originally designed for the University of La Rioja, with six weights named after grape varieties from the Rioja wine region.
description: Designed by Andreu Balius between 2008 and 2015, Rioja draws on early 20th-century geometric and grotesque models while maintaining a clean, disciplined structure. The uppercase is characteristically simple and rectangular, broken by details such as the J descending below the baseline, while the lowercase prioritises group harmony and readability at text sizes. Six weights run from the light Viura through to the heavy Maturana, each taking its name from a grape variety native to the region.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Rioja as a family that combines typography with the world of wine, originally created for the identity of the University of La Rioja in Logroño and refined for commercial release in 2014. Each weight is named after a characteristic grape variety from the Rioja region, and the design is inspired by the geometric and grotesque styles of the early 20th century. They highlight its versatility for both text and signage, where legibility and visual impact are equally important.

name: Ravals
classification_notes: Ravals is a casual brush script typeface designed for display use, drawing direct inspiration from 1950s and 60s brush lettering styles. Its informal, spontaneous character and smooth character connections place it squarely within the brush script subcategory.
summary: A casual display brush script by Ricard Garcia, rooted in 1950s and 60s lettering and named after Barcelona's Raval neighbourhood.
description: Drawn by Ricard Garcia between 2019 and 2020, Ravals takes its cues from mid-century brush lettering to produce an informal yet consistent texture suited to display work. The typeface includes default, initial, medial, final and isolated character forms, allowing smooth connections across letter combinations with additional alternates for specific pairings. Its extended Latin character set covers a broad range of languages, from Central European to Polynesian scripts.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Ravals as a funky script typeface for display sizes, inspired by brush lettering from the 1950s and 60s and well matched to music of that era. They position its spontaneous design as a tribute to the vitality and rhythm — known locally as Ravalejar — of Barcelona's Raval neighbourhood. The typeface achieves its lively texture through default, initial, medial, final and isolated character forms, with alternative constructions for specific letter combinations to ensure fluid connections.

name: SV Caps
classification_notes: An all-caps display family with heavily decorated and ornamented letterforms drawing on mid-century popular music aesthetics; the range of inline, scratch, stripes, waves and solid variants places it firmly in the decorative display category.
summary: A large all-caps display family with mid-century popular music references, offering solid, scratched, striped and wave-filled letterform variants alongside ornament fonts.
description: Each subfamily in SV Caps takes its name from a popular music genre or dance style, from Bebop and Bolero through to Mambo and Twist, and each comes in multiple treatments including solid, scratch, stripes and waves. Ornament and letter fonts accompany many styles, enabling layered or standalone use. Twenty stylistic sets and extensive OpenType alternates give designers considerable control over individual letterforms.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic present SV Caps as a wide-ranging decorative caps family organised around mid-century music and dance references, with each style available in solid, textured and ornamented variants and supported by a comprehensive set of OpenType features including twenty stylistic sets.

name: SV Centenary
designer: Joan Trochut, Andreu Balius, Ricard Garcia
classification_notes: A modular display type system derived from Joan Trochut's mid-century SuperVeloz printing system, with ornamental and constructed letterforms intended for large expressive headlines rather than text setting.
summary: A 12-style display collection honouring Joan Trochut's modular SuperVeloz system, built for expressive large-scale headline work with textured and patterned variants.
description: Released in 2020 to mark the centenary of Joan Trochut's birth, this collection extends Typerepublic's 2004 digitisation of the SuperVeloz modular printing system into 12 ready-made display styles. Styles are named after mid-century musical genres and range from solid and scratch cuts through wave and stripe variants, each offering alternate forms via stylistic sets. Won a Gold award at the European Design Awards 2021.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe SV Centenary as a tribute to Joan Trochut (1920–1980), building on the modular SuperVeloz system digitised in 2004. The 12 new styles feature extended character sets and alternative forms drawn from Trochut's original prints, designed to simplify typesetting and perform at large display sizes for expressive headlines.

name: SuperVeloz
designer: Joan Trochut, Andreu Balius, Àlex Trochut
classification_notes: SuperVeloz is a modular type system of combinable metal pieces rather than complete characters, placing it firmly in the display category as a highly decorative and constructional system with no single fixed letterform.
summary: A digital revival of Joan Trochut's 1942 modular metal type system, in which over 200 combinable pieces allow the construction of any letterform or symbol.
description: Originally produced in 1942 by Fundición Tipográfica Iranzo in Barcelona, SuperTipo Veloz replaced complete characters with combinable metal modules, freeing printers from the constraints of conventional lead type. Digitised in 2004 by Andreu Balius and Àlex Trochut, the revival comprises nine styles across Basic, Complement, RasgosVeloz and two Caps families designed for drop caps, logotypes and letterheads. The system won the Type Directors Club Excellence in Type Design award in 2005.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe SuperVeloz as a collection of type modules created by Joan Trochut for the José Iranzo foundry in Barcelona in the early 1940s, and revived digitally by Andreu Balius and Àlex Trochut in 2004. They present it as a strictly integral modular system in which individual metal pieces represent parts of characters rather than whole letters, allowing printers to combine over 200 module designs to construct any letter, illustration or symbol in any language.

name: Tubqal
classification_notes: Tubqal is a multiscript humanist sans-serif family designed to harmonise Latin, Arabic and Tifinagh scripts within a coherent typographic system, with no apparent serif construction.
summary: A tri-script family covering Latin, Arabic and Tifinagh, originally commissioned by the Khatt Foundation as part of a multiscript typographic research programme focused on the Maghrib region.
description: Developed from a Khatt Foundation commission within the Typographic Matchmaking in the Maghrib 3.0 project, Tubqal integrates three scripts into a coherent family of six weights with italics throughout. The design aims to reflect the linguistic diversity of the Maghrib, creating typographic harmony between Latin, Maghribi-based Arabic and the Tifinagh script used for Amazigh languages. The Tifinagh styles are offered free of charge to non-profit associations supporting Amazigh culture.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Tubqal as a tri-script type family evolving from an original commission by the Khatt Foundation as part of the Typographic Matchmaking in the Maghrib 3.0 project. The initiative is the third edition of the foundation's multiscript typographic research programme, aimed at fostering cultural dialogue and strengthening local design skills across the Maghrib region.

name: Taüll
classification_notes: Taüll is explicitly inspired by ancient blackletter scripts and alphabets carved in stone, featuring bold angular capitals with humanistic irregularities typical of Southern European gothic lettering traditions.
summary: A blackletter display face by Andreu Balius rooted in stone-carved Pyrenean lettering, offering bold angular capitals with humanistic irregularities across a single weight.
description: Designed in 2007, Taüll draws on ancient blackletter and lapidary scripts from the Southern European Pyrenean tradition, evoking Romanesque churches and mountain villages. Its capital letters are sharp and commanding, tempered by subtle humanistic irregularities that soften the severity typical of northern Gothic forms. A single Regular weight covers an extensive Latin language range including localised forms for Catalan and Dutch.
foundry_blurb: Typerepublic describe Taüll as a typeface inspired by ancient blackletter scripts, intended to bridge medieval tradition with modern digital contexts. Rooted in a Southern European aesthetic, it evokes the Pyrenean landscape of small villages, Romanesque churches and shadowy forests. Its letterforms are based on classical stone-carved alphabets, combining sharp angles with humanistic irregularities to balance historical character with contemporary legibility.
